nodes.name: Number Theory
nodes.description: Properties of integers, prime numbers, and famous number sequences

items.prompt: What are numbers called that are equal to the sum of their proper divisors, such as 6, 28, 496, and 8128?
items.explanation:
6 = 1+2+3, 28 = 1+2+4+7+14, 496, 8128 are the first four
Known to the ancient Greeks — Euclid proved that 2^(p−1)(2^p − 1) generates even perfect numbers when 2^p − 1 is prime
Only 52 perfect numbers are known (as of 2024); all are even — whether odd perfect numbers exist is one of the oldest unsolved problems
Nicomachus (c. 100 CE) classified numbers as deficient, perfect, or abundant
St. Augustine wrote that God created the world in 6 days because 6 is a perfect number
Each known perfect number ends in 6 or 8

items.prompt: What are primes of the form 2^p − 1 called, named after a 17th-century French Minim friar?
items.explanation:
Named after Marin Mersenne (1588–1648), a Minim friar who studied which values of p yield primes
First few: 3 (p=2), 7 (p=3), 31 (p=5), 127 (p=7)
All known even perfect numbers correspond to a Mersenne prime via Euclid's formula
The Great Internet Mersenne Prime Search (GIMPS), founded 1996, uses volunteer computers to find new ones
The largest known prime is almost always a Mersenne prime — as of 2024 the record is 2^136,279,841 − 1 (over 41 million digits)
Only 52 Mersenne primes are known; whether infinitely many exist is unsolved

items.prompt: What are pairs of primes called that differ by exactly 2, such as (3, 5), (5, 7), (11, 13), and (17, 19)?
items.explanation:
The twin prime conjecture states there are infinitely many such pairs — unproven since first proposed
The pair (5, 7) is the only case where twin primes overlap (sharing the prime 5 with (3, 5))
In 2013, Yitang Zhang proved there are infinitely many prime pairs with gap less than 70 million — a breakthrough from obscurity
The Polymath project then reduced Zhang's bound; James Maynard brought it to 600
As of 2024, the bound is 246, but reducing it to 2 (proving the twin prime conjecture) remains open
Largest known twin primes have over 388,000 digits

items.prompt: What are pairs of numbers called where each equals the sum of the other's proper divisors, such as 220 and 284?
items.explanation:
220's proper divisors sum to 284; 284's proper divisors sum to 220
Known to the Pythagoreans, who considered them symbols of friendship
Thabit ibn Qurra (9th century) discovered a formula generating amicable pairs — the first new pair since antiquity
Euler found over 60 pairs in the 18th century
The second-smallest pair (1184, 1210) was missed until 1866 when a 16-year-old Italian student found it
Over 1.2 billion pairs are now known, but whether infinitely many exist is unproven
Arab mathematicians used them in talismans and love potions

items.prompt: What ancient algorithm finds all prime numbers up to a given limit by iteratively marking the multiples of each prime as composite?
items.explanation:
Created by Eratosthenes of Cyrene (c. 276–194 BCE) — the same scholar who calculated Earth's circumference
Start with 2; cross out all multiples of 2, then 3, then 5, etc. — the surviving numbers are prime
Only need to check up to √n — for primes up to 100, sieving through 2, 3, 5, 7 suffices
Still used as a benchmark in computer science; modern variants (Sieve of Atkin, segmented sieves) are faster
The simplest correct algorithm for generating a list of primes
Remains one of the most famous algorithms from antiquity alongside Euclid's algorithm for GCD
items.cardFront: Cross out multiples to find primes

items.prompt: What number, arising from a Ramsey theory problem about hypercube colorings, was once the largest ever used in a mathematical proof?
items.explanation:
Arose from a problem by Ronald Graham and Bruce Rothschild (1971) in Ramsey theory about coloring edges of hypercubes
So large that ordinary notation (exponents, towers of exponents) cannot express it — requires Knuth's up-arrow notation
Even the number of digits is itself incomprehensibly large
The observable universe couldn't contain a decimal representation — there aren't enough particles
Held the Guinness World Record for the largest number ever used in a mathematical proof (since surpassed)
Its last 500 digits are known (ends in ...2464195387) even though the full number is unknowable

items.prompt: What number is the smallest expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways (1³+12³ and 9³+10³), famously identified by Ramanujan from a taxi cab number?
items.explanation:
1³ + 12³ = 1 + 1728 = 1729, and 9³ + 10³ = 729 + 1000 = 1729
G.H. Hardy visited Ramanujan in hospital and remarked his taxi number, 1729, was "rather dull"
Ramanujan instantly replied it was the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways
Now called "taxicab numbers" — the nth taxicab number is the smallest expressible as the sum of two positive cubes in n ways
Srinivasa Ramanujan was self-taught from India; Hardy called their collaboration "the one romantic incident in my life"
Ramanujan died in 1920 at age 32, leaving notebooks that mathematicians are still mining for results

items.prompt: What class of numbers, including π and e, are not roots of any polynomial equation with integer coefficients?
items.explanation:
Defined as numbers that are not algebraic — they satisfy no polynomial equation with integer coefficients
π was proved transcendental by Ferdinand von Lindemann in 1882, settling the ancient problem of "squaring the circle" as impossible
e was proved transcendental by Charles Hermite in 1873
Almost all real numbers are transcendental — algebraic numbers are countable, but transcendentals are uncountable
Despite being "almost all" numbers, proving a specific number is transcendental is extremely difficult
Whether π + e or πe is transcendental remains unknown
items.cardFront: Not a root of any integer polynomial

items.prompt: What theorem describes how prime numbers thin out among larger integers, approximating their count up to n as n/ln(n)?
items.explanation:
The number of primes up to n is approximately n/ln(n) — primes thin out but never stop
Conjectured by Gauss (age 15!) and Legendre in the 1790s based on prime tables
Proved independently by Jacques Hadamard and Charles-Jean de la Vallée Poussin in 1896
The proof used complex analysis — surprising since primes are integers and the proof involves imaginary numbers
A sharper estimate uses the logarithmic integral Li(n) instead of n/ln(n)
Erdős and Selberg found an "elementary" proof in 1949 (no complex analysis), leading to a famous priority dispute

items.prompt: What four-digit number is the fixed point reached when you repeatedly sort a number's digits in descending and ascending order and subtract?
items.explanation:
Take any four-digit number (not all digits the same): arrange digits descending, subtract ascending, repeat — always reaches 6174 in at most 7 steps
Example: 3524 → 5432−2345 = 3087 → 8730−0378 = 8352 → 8532−2358 = 6174
Discovered by Indian mathematician D.R. Kaprekar in 1949
6174 is a fixed point: 7641−1467 = 6174
For three-digit numbers, the analogous constant is 495
Kaprekar was a schoolteacher who worked in recreational mathematics — initially dismissed by professionals

items.prompt: What fundamental discovery, attributed to the Pythagoreans around 500 BCE, showed that the square root of 2 cannot be expressed as a fraction?
alternates: irrationality of the square root of 2
items.explanation:
The diagonal of a unit square has length √2, which cannot be written as a ratio of integers
Legend says Hippasus of Metapontum discovered this c. 500 BCE and was drowned by fellow Pythagoreans for revealing it
The proof by contradiction (assume √2 = p/q in lowest terms, derive both must be even — contradiction) is one of the most elegant in mathematics
Shattered the Pythagorean belief that all quantities are ratios of whole numbers
Led to the concept of irrational numbers — now known to include √3, √5, and all non-perfect-square roots
The crisis it caused is sometimes called the "first foundational crisis" in mathematics

items.prompt: What unsolved conjecture states that repeatedly applying "if even divide by 2, if odd multiply by 3 and add 1" to any positive integer always eventually reaches 1?
items.explanation:
Also called the 3n+1 problem, the Syracuse problem, and Ulam's conjecture
Example: 7→22→11→34→17→52→26→13→40→20→10→5→16→8→4→2→1
Posed by Lothar Collatz in 1937; remains unsolved despite its simplicity
Paul Erdős said "Mathematics is not yet ready for such problems"
Verified by computer for all starting values up to at least 2.36 × 10²¹
Terence Tao proved in 2019 that "almost all" Collatz sequences reach values close to 1, the strongest result so far
The sequence for 27 takes 111 steps and reaches a peak of 9232 before descending to 1
